title: Chapter Five: What Grows in the Dark
content: She was going to escape. Not because she wanted to—though God, she wanted to—but because people were going to die if she didn't.

Aria paced the narrow cell, her bare feet silent on the cool stone. Three steps to the door. Pivot. Three steps back. The geometry of her confinement had worn a groove in her mind, a channel her thoughts kept falling into no matter how she tried to climb out. Nightshade in the supply routes. Uranium in the springs. Iris's fingers beside her jaw, close enough to feel the heat.
Move, she told herself. Think later. Move now.

She pressed her ear to the door. The corridor beyond was silent—no footsteps, no murmured conversation. The guards changed at moonrise and again at the small hours, a pattern she'd tracked through the sounds that filtered through the stone: the clink of harness, the scrape of boots, the low exchange of passwords she couldn't quite catch. The window between shifts was narrow. Maybe twenty minutes. Maybe less.
She needed help.

Prisca came when Aria scratched at the door like a rat—three short bursts, two long, the signal they'd improvised during Aria's second week when Prisca had smuggled her extra bread and the knowledge that the night watchman drank too much fermented grain.
The girl's face appeared in the gap beneath the door, one eye dark and wide in the lamplight.
"Aria? What's—"
"Can you get in here?"

A pause. The sound of a key turning. The door swung inward, and Prisca slipped through like water through a crack, closing it behind her. She was in her sleeping clothes—thin linen, bare feet, her hair loose around her shoulders. She looked very young.
"I have to get out," Aria said.
Prisca's eye twitched. "Out of the room? I can talk to the—"
"Out of the Mirror. Out of the settlement. Tonight, if I can. Tomorrow at the latest."
The silence that followed was thick enough to chew. Prisca stared at her, and Aria watched the girl's throat work—swallowing, processing, choosing.
"Is this about the water?" Prisca whispered.

"It's about the water. It's about the supply routes. It's about the fact that Iris just ordered enough nightshade concentrated into the western passage to kill hundreds of people, Prisca. Slowly. Over weeks. They'll think it's sickness, and they'll keep drinking, and they'll keep dying, and Iris will stand in her garden and call it *necessary*."
The words came out harder than she meant. Prisca flinched like she'd been struck.
"I'm sorry," Aria said. "I'm sorry. I know—I know she's—"
"She's everything here." Prisca's voice was barely audible. "She's the reason any of us are alive."

"She's also the reason some of them *down there* won't be. Soon." Aria grabbed Prisca's hands—they were cold, the fingers calloused from reed-splitting and page-turning. "I have to warn them. And I have to find out what's causing the contamination leak before it kills everyone up here too. I can't do either of those things in this room."
"You'll die out there."
"Maybe."
"There are things in the dark—"

"I've lived in the dark, Prisca. I came *from* the dark." A lie, technically—she'd come from a climate lab in the 21st century, not a post-Collapse tunnel system—but true enough in spirit. "I know what rust tastes like. I know what a failing air filter smells like. I'll manage."

Prisca's hands trembled in hers. The girl was shaking—Aria could feel it running through her like current through wire. But her jaw was set, and her dark eyes held something that looked less like fear and more like the moment before a leap.

"I want to know what's below," Prisca said. "I've wanted to know since Solon first told me there were people under the Garden. I've dreamed about it. The old ones came from below, didn't they? Before the Garden. Before the mirrors. They came up from somewhere, and nobody talks about it, and I—"
She stopped. Breathed.
"I'll help you."

Solon was awake, because Solon was always awake. He sat in his archive like a spider at the center of a web of fragments, an oil lamp burning low beside his knee, his trembling hands resting on a map spread across his lap.

The map was old. Not pre-Collapse old—Aria could tell by the fiber composition of the paper, the vegetable-based ink—but old enough. Fifty years, maybe seventy. It showed the Mirror of the Sky in cross-section: the surface settlement, the garden terraces, the spring channels, and below them all, a network of tunnels and shafts rendered in faded red ink, branching like veins.

"Maintenance access," Solon said, his voice rust and honey in the dimness. "Built before the Garden was a garden, when this place was still a *facility*. That door you found in Iris's private plot—it connects to the same system. But you won't get through that way. She has it watched."

He tapped the map with a finger that shook like a leaf. "Here. Behind the archive. There is a grate—iron, half-rusted through—where the old ventilation shaft meets the storage cellar. It was a service entrance once. The Under-dwellers' paths branch from it."

Aria leaned closer. The tunnel system on the map was a tangle, but she could trace the logic of it—main arteries splitting into capillaries, some marked with symbols she didn't recognize, others annotated in Solon's cramped hand.
"You've been down there?" she asked.

"Once. Forty years ago, when my legs could still carry me and my hands could still grip a rope." His eyes went distant—not vacant, but *deep*, like a well with no bottom. "I went as far as the first junction. I heard machines. I heard breathing that wasn't human. I came back up and told no one."
"Why?"

"Because Iris asked me not to." He said it simply, without bitterness. "She was young, then. Not the Priestess—just a girl with sharp eyes and a voice that made people listen. She said the below was death, and I believed her because I had heard it breathing."
"You don't believe her now."

Solon looked at her. The lamplight carved his face into topography—crags and hollows, a landscape of years. "I believe she believes it. I also believe she is wrong. There is a difference between death and *something that kills*, Aria. The underground is not dead. It is *hungry*. And Iris has been feeding it for eleven years and calling the feeding *peace*."
He folded the map with excruciating care and pressed it into her hands.

"The Garden has roots that go deeper than anyone knows," he said. The phrase had the weight of repetition—words he'd carried so long they'd worn smooth. "If you go down, you will find what Iris has been hiding. But it will also find you."
Aria tucked the map inside her shirt. The paper was cool against her sternum, crackling faintly with each breath.
"Solon. The logo on the door—the M inside the gear. Masterson Industrial. Do you know it?"
The old man's hands stilled. Not the usual tremor-pause—this was different. This was *stillness*, absolute and animal, the kind that preceded flight or fight.
"Moloch," he said.
The word dropped into the room like a stone into deep water.
"That's not—I said Masterson. The corporation."

"Moloch is what they called themselves. Before the branding, before the public relations, before the logos and the shareholder reports. In the corrupted files—the ones where the data decays into noise but the names survive—that word appears again and again. *Moloch proceeds.* *Moloch consumes.* *Moloch endures.*" Solon's voice had dropped to a whisper, as though the walls themselves might be listening. "The corporations that ate the sky, Aria. There were many names on the surface. Masterson. Helios Dynamics. Evergreen Solutions. But underneath—all underneath—*Moloch*. The same entity. The same hunger."
"That's not how corporations work. They compete. They—"

"They *ate*," Solon said, and the word came out fierce, breaking through the tremor like bone through skin. "They ate the sky and the sea and the soil, and when there was nothing left to eat, they ate each other, and when there was nothing left of each other, they *went to sleep*. They did not die with the old world, child. They just—waited. Like seeds in frozen ground. Like bones in dry soil."
His hands had stopped shaking entirely. That frightened Aria more than anything else.

"The Heart that beats below us," Solon continued, quieter now. "The machine I heard breathing forty years ago. I believe—I have believed since I first read the name—that it is *theirs*. Not built by the people who live there now. Built *for* something. For something that has not yet woken."
The oil lamp guttered. Shadows swam across the fragments on the shelves—pages and tablets and cracked screens, all of them whispering the same word in a language Aria was only beginning to hear.
*Moloch.*

"Go," Solon said. "Go tonight. The new moon. The guards are slowest when they cannot see the sky—they think the dark protects them. Perhaps it once did." He reached out and gripped her wrist—his fingers weak, his grip desperate. "Find the truth, Aria. And if you cannot bring it back to us—burn it. Burn it so that it cannot wake."
The new moon rose like a wound that wouldn't close—invisible, but felt. The air pressure shifted, and the Garden's wind-vanes creaked in the darkness like things trying to speak.
Aria waited.

She'd dressed in Prisca's spare clothes: dark linen, close-fitting, soft-soled shoes that wouldn't click on stone. The map was tucked against her chest. A small satchel held a water bottle, a handful of dried apricots, and the bone knife from Iris's rose-cutting table—stolen two days ago, the only weapon she could find that wouldn't be missed immediately.
Great, she thought. My first prison break and I'm armed with gardening supplies.

Prisca materialized at her door like a trick of the dark. She'd braided her hair tight and smeared her face with ash from the distillery hearth—she looked older, harder, less like a girl and more like a soldier playing dress-up.
"The guard's moving," Prisca whispered. "We have maybe fifteen minutes before the next one rounds the corner."
They moved.

The corridors of the Mirror were white stone and shadow, solar lamps dimmed to their lowest setting, the air cool and smelling of roses. Always roses. Even here, in the guts of the place, Iris's signature scent clung to the walls like a claim of ownership. Aria found herself breathing through her mouth to avoid it.

The archive was dark. Solon was gone—moved to the eastern quarter, he'd said, so that no one would think to look for him near the tunnel. But he'd left the grate loose, the iron bars loosened by years of rust and one old man's patient, trembling work.

Aria pulled the grate aside. The shaft behind it was black—not dark, *black*, the color of nothing, the color of the world before someone thought to invent light. A current of air rose from it, carrying the smell of rust and wet stone and something older: mineral, electric, the deep-time scent of the earth's bones.
"I'll go first," Aria said.
"I know." Prisca's voice wavered, then steadied. "I'm right behind you."
Aria lowered herself into the shaft.

The darkness swallowed her like a mouth. Her feet found a rung—iron, corroded but solid—and she descended, hand over hand, her shoulder blades scraping the walls. The stone was cold and slick with condensation. Rust flaked under her fingers, orange-black, coming away on her palms like dried blood. The air grew denser as she went down, heavier, pressing against her eardrums.
Water dripped somewhere below. A rhythmic sound—not random, not natural. *Mechanical.*

She counted rungs. Twelve. Twenty. Thirty. The shaft narrowed. Her hips caught on a protrusion and she gasped, bit down on the sound, kept moving. Prisca's breathing echoed above her—quick, shallow, brave.

Forty rungs. The shaft opened into a horizontal tunnel, barely tall enough to stand, the ceiling a lattice of old pipes and corroded brackets. The floor was packed earth overlaid with metal grating that shifted under her weight with a sound like a skeleton stretching.

Aria clicked on the small lamp Solon had given her—a hand-cranked LED, pre-Collapse manufacture, its beam the color of weak sunlight. The tunnel stretched ahead into impossible distance, walls glistening with moisture, the pipes running into darkness like veins toward a heart she could almost hear.
"The Under-dwellers' paths," she breathed.
"Which way?" Prisca dropped down beside her, landing hard, her ash-streaked face tight with controlled terror.
Aria pulled the map from her shirt. In the lamplight, Solon's careful annotations jumped out: *first junction—left to supply routes, right to deep access.* She traced the line with her finger.
"Left. The supply routes. If we follow them, we can reach the western passage and—"
The alarm split the night like a scream.

Not the gentle chiming of the Garden's dawn-call—this was *sharper*, a brazen howl that ricocheted off the tunnel walls and drove into Aria's skull like a chisel. She stumbled, caught herself on the wall, felt the rust bite into her palm.
"That's the perimeter alarm," Prisca said, and her voice had gone very small.
Then the ground *shook*.

Not the tremor of a collapsing shaft—not the settling of old stone. This was *concussive*, a deep-chested *whump* that traveled up through Aria's feet and into her spine, rattling her teeth in their sockets. Dust cascaded from the ceiling. The pipes overhead groaned, a sound like a dying animal.
A second explosion. A third. Closer.
Voices. Screaming. Not the measured calm of the Garden's daily rhythms—raw, animal sounds, chopped into fragments by distance and stone. Something was happening above them. Something *big*.
"The Iron Folk," Prisca whispered, and her eyes were white-ringed with terror. "They're coming *up*."
Aria's mind raced. The supply route poison. The nightshade in the western passage. If the underground people were already coming up—if they'd found out somehow, or if they'd been pushed far enough—
*They're not here to rescue anyone.*

The thought landed with the weight of a collapsing ceiling. The Iron Folk—Nova's people, Solon had called them—wouldn't know or care that Aria was trying to warn them. They'd see a surface-dweller, one of Iris's *guests*, and they'd—

A fourth explosion. This one close enough that the shockwave punched through the tunnel and knocked Aria sideways into the wall. Her lamp fell, struck the grating, spun like a dying star, and went out.

Total darkness. The smell of dust and blood and burning. The screaming from above—closer now, resolving into words she could almost understand: *—breach the eastern wall—* and *—take the water station—* and something else, a voice deeper than the others, cutting through the chaos like a blade through smoke.
Prisca's hand found hers in the dark—fingers interlacing, grip so tight it hurt.
"Which way?" the girl gasped. "Aria, which *way*?"

Back was the settlement—burning now, by the sound of it, the Garden's perfect white walls cracking open like eggshells. Forward was the tunnel—black, unknown, leading down toward something that Solon said breathed like a machine and might be waiting to wake.
Aria squeezed Prisca's hand. Made the choice.
"Forward. Down. *Move.*"
They ran.

The tunnel swallowed them. The darkness was absolute, broken only by the thunder of their footsteps on the metal grating and the ragged concert of their breathing. Behind them, the alarms wailed and the explosions continued—*boom, boom, boom*—each one sending tremors through the walls, shaking loose rust and dust that fell into their eyes and mouths. Aria spat grit and kept running, one hand on the wall, the other locked around Prisca's fingers.

The tunnel widened. The ceiling lifted. The air changed—warmer now, and tinged with a smell she recognized from the underground she'd never actually lived in: machine oil, ozone, the metallic tang of something running hot.
And smoke.

She could *see* the smoke, which meant there was light ahead—not the weak beam of her lost lamp, but something brighter, something *orange*, flickering against the tunnel walls like the heartbeat of the earth itself.
They burst out of the tunnel mouth into a space that shouldn't have existed.

A junction. A *cavern*, carved from the rock by hands and machines long dead, its ceiling lost in shadow, its walls laced with conduits and cables that sparked and spat in the firelight. The floor was chaos—rubble, collapsed scaffolding, the body of something mechanical that had died mid-motion. And through the smoke, from a second tunnel mouth across the cavern, came the *sound*: heavy footsteps, clanging metal, voices raised in a language Aria didn't speak but understood in her bones. War-language. Raid-language.
The *forward* had led them directly into the path of the Iron raid.

Aria yanked Prisca behind a collapsed beam. They pressed flat against the stone, hearts hammering, breath held, as the sound grew closer—footsteps, the scrape of metal on rock, the hum of something *powered*, something that vibrated the air in Aria's chest like a bass note she couldn't unhear.

A voice cut through the smoke—not shouting, not commanding. *Singing.* Low and steady, a rhythm that matched the footsteps, that matched the hum, that matched the pulse of fire in Aria's vision. A war-song, maybe, or a working chant, or just the sound a person made when they were walking into hell and refusing to flinch.
The figure emerged from the smoke.

Silver hair, cropped close to the skull, catching firelight like a halo made of blades. A face sharp enough to cut—high cheekbones, dark eyes reflecting orange, an expression carved from the same stone as the tunnel walls: hard, patient, *terrible*. And below the face, below the scorched-leather shoulders and the combat rig hung with tools Aria couldn't name, a left arm that stopped at the elbow and continued in *chrome*—articulated metal, scorched and scratched, servos whining faintly, the forearm ending in a plasma cutter that glowed orange-white in the smoky dark, its tip still humming with heat.

The figure stopped. The singing stopped. The chrome arm rose—slowly, casually, like a person raising a hand to shade their eyes—and the plasma cutter swept the cavern, its light carving shadows from the smoke.
It swept past the collapsed beam.
Came back.
Looked directly at Aria.
The underground had arrived, and they were not here to rescue anyone.
